examinerReportSynthesis:
# Examiner Report Synthesis — MW Practical Exam 2017–2025

Synthesized from 8 practical examiner reports and 5 chief examiner reports across 2017–2025.

## 1. Mark Allocation (Trending Over Time)

The exam is shifting away from identification and toward analytical competencies:

| Year | Identification | Quality/Maturity | Winemaking | Commercial |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| 2021 | 43% | 22% | 20% | 15% |
| 2022 | 46% | 24% | 21% | 9% |
| 2023 | 39% | 37% | ~16% | ~8% |
| 2024 | 39% | — | — | — |
| 2025 | — | 20%+ (quality alone) | — | — |

**Key quote (2024)**: "The MW practical exam is a theory exam with a tasting. This is truer today than ever before."

**Implication for mock exams**: Questions must allocate marks consistently with this trend. Identification should be ~35–45% of marks per question. Quality/maturity/winemaking/commercial should collectively be 55–65%.

## 2. The Seven Cardinal Rules (Recurring Across All Reports)

### Rule 1: Reasoning > Identification
Every single report (2017–2025) emphasizes this. Selected quotes:
- "More marks can be given when the conclusion is wrong, as we can then see and reward intelligent thinking." (2019)
- "Over half did not get a single origin exactly right, however many still managed 5-6 marks out of 8 if their reasoning was sound." (2025)
- "The argument is as important as the conclusion and is the key to passing identification questions." (2022)
- "A wrong answer yields more marks than an answer that is unfinished, so whatever you do: Make a choice." (2021)

### Rule 2: Quality Must Be Contextualized
Every report flags "good" or "very good" without context as earning zero or near-zero marks.
- Must use official classification levels where they exist (Grand Cru Classé, Cru Bourgeois, DOCG, Pradikat, VORS, etc.)
- Must use price points to corroborate quality position
- Must position wine on a quality ladder relative to its origin/peers
- "Even if we don't ask for it specifically, we do expect an official quality level if there is one and it is relevant." (2025)
- "The key is to try to communicate from an MW to an MW where on a quality scale this wine would be placed." (2023)
- One student "used the phrase 'good quality wine' for all twelve wines, yielding a lot of zero marks." (2021)

### Rule 3: No Shoehorning
Named explicitly in 2023, 2024, 2025; described in every earlier report.
- Candidates decide identity first, then force tasting notes to fit
- "There was a lot of shoehorning on this year's paper two exam, and ultimately, it led to the failure of many candidates." (2025)
- The antidote is honest structural assessment: read alcohol, acidity, tannin, RS accurately FIRST, then deduce identity

### Rule 4: Answer the Question as Asked
Flagged in every single report. Candidates routinely:
- Answer the question they prepared for, not the one in front of them
- Miss sub-parts (e.g., "opportunities AND challenges" — many answer only opportunities)
- Waste time on aspects not asked for (e.g., describing traditional method deduction when told all wines are traditional method)
- Confuse "compare and contrast" with "describe each separately"

### Rule 5: Maturity Has Four Required Elements
Explicitly defined (2023): "For maturity there needs to be an assessment of (1) the current age of the wine, (2) whether it is ready to drink now or would benefit from further ageing, (3) how long it might improve for, and (4) how long it is likely to hold before it declines."
- Vague statements like "matured for many years" earn minimal marks
- Specific timeframes expected
- "Best answers described both positive and negative evolution trajectories." (2023)

### Rule 6: Commercial Must Be Specific and Global
- Channels: on-trade vs off-trade, specialist vs supermarket, by-the-glass vs list
- Geography: domestic market, export markets, global perspective
- Price point: realistic and evidence-based
- Competitive set: what other wines compete for this shelf/list position
- "We expect students to tell us where we can find this wine. On or off trade? Specialist or supermarket? Michelin Star restaurant or the local pub?" (2025)
- "Too many talked about steakhouses as the answer to commercial appeal." (2022, 2023, 2024)
- "Rote responses" are explicitly penalized
- Food pairings "rarely rewarded with any marks" (2022)

### Rule 7: Structural Evidence Is the Foundation
- Accurate alcohol, acidity, tannin, RS assessment is the non-negotiable starting point
- "Hard evidence like alcohol and sugar are often more reliable than the flavour profile." (2025)
- "One potential pitfall in paper two is candidates relying too much on their noses." (2025)
- Inaccurate structural calls cascade through the entire answer

## 3. What Examiners Reward (Positive Patterns)

### Funnelling technique
Explicitly endorsed by name in every report from 2017 onward. Structure:
1. Consider all evidence from the glass
2. List 2-3 plausible options
3. Argue for and against each
4. Narrow to a conclusion with reasoning
5. State conclusion clearly (ideally at the top of the answer)

### Enthusiasm and conviction
- "When candidates can convey the genuine pleasure they experience in tasting a 40-year-old Tawny, that enthusiasm is shared by those reading the paper." (2025)
- "It's essential that enthusiasm and a genuine love of great wines comes through in your answers." (2018)
- "Confidence and authority" are rewarded; hedging and vagueness are not

### Cross-referencing wines within flights
- Using one wine to unlock another within the same question
- "Where you have more than one wine to help you, one of them will show enough character to open the door." (2017)
- "The best candidates remembered to draw on all four wines in their arguments." (2025)

### Honest engagement with difficult wines
- Describing what is actually in the glass, even when the wine is unidentifiable
- "Even if candidates don't know the wine, they should trust their palates." (2021)
- "A lot of points could have been gained from simply describing what was in the glass even if the region was unknown." (2024)

### Linking winemaking to sensory evidence
- Not just listing techniques, but connecting them to what can be tasted
- "The best candidates were thorough in their winemaking observations and tied these back to what they could taste in the glass." (2018)
- Chronological approach to winemaking endorsed (reception → fermentation → maturation → bottling)

## 4. What Examiners Penalize (Error Patterns)

### Theory errors that undermine confidence
- "Tawny Port aged in a solera system" (2025)
- "Amontillado Sherry has 14.5% alcohol" (2025)
- "Malolactic before fermentation, oak ageing before crushing" (2021)
- "Sauternes fermented at 16°C in stainless steel" (2024)
- "Meursault, Grand Cru" (2017)
- "Pouilly Fuissé in the Loire" (2017)
- These "howlers" sink borderline papers

### Copy-paste / repetitive answers
- "An increasing number of answers appear repetitive and are clearly using cut and paste. It is unlikely examiners have chosen two wines with the same attributes." (2023)
- "Creates considerable doubt in the mind of the reader." (2024)

### Over-reliance on aromatics for reds
- "One potential pitfall in paper two is candidates relying too much on their noses." (2025)
- Structural elements (tannin, body, alcohol, acidity) are often more diagnostic than aromatic profiles

### Phantom oak detection
- "Many more answers found oak when it wasn't there than vice versa." (2018)
- Conversely, "it was noticeable how many candidates missed the obvious new oak use." (2023)

### Failure to recognize quality extremes
- Both overrating simple wines and underrating great wines
- "Calling a Côtes du Rhône a Châteauneuf-du-Pape, or describing the Ruby Port as a vintage Port, undermines the examiner's confidence." (2025)
- "Hardly anyone was sufficiently effusive about the quality of wine 12" — a Chilean Cabernet whose quality was downplayed due to origin bias (2018)

### Unprofessional language
- Terms like "stonking," "icon," "Goldilocks" are explicitly criticized (2018)
- Bullet points "rarely make for a strong argument" (2019)

## 5. Paper-Specific Insights

### Paper 1 (Whites)
- Acidity/RS interplay is a persistent weak spot: "A number seem to struggle with reading acidity, especially when there is some residual sugar involved." (2025)
- MLF absence is as important as MLF presence: "Their absence is as much of a winemaking choice as their presence." (2022)
- Oak detection on whites requires precision (lees, MLF, barrel size, new vs old)

### Paper 2 (Reds)
- Over-reliance on nose is the biggest trap
- Classic regions must be known cold: Bordeaux (commune-level), Burgundy (village+cru), Barolo/Barbaresco, Rioja, Northern/Southern Rhône
- Candidates default to famous appellations (CdP, Hermitage) even when they're not present
- "Where in the northern Rhone is going to produce a wine with the high alcohol and breadth of wine 8?" (2017)

### Paper 3 (Sparkling/Fortified/Sweet/Rosé/Oxidative)
- Consistently the weakest paper and the deciding factor for borderline candidates
- Theory knowledge is non-negotiable: "It is very hard, if not impossible, to pass the MW exam without knowing how Madeira or Sherry is made." (2025)
- Production method questions require explaining HOW (e.g., how fermentation was stopped), not just WHAT
- Official quality levels must be cited: Beerenauslese, VORS, Reserva, etc.
- Accurate alcohol and sugar estimation is critical — candidates who get these wrong cascade into wrong identifications

## 6. Exam Design Principles (For Mock Exam Writer)

### Questions are designed to allow marks even when identification fails
- "The questions were structured in a way that prevented misidentification of origin from affecting candidates' performances too adversely." (2019)
- "Most of the marks were for discussing quality, maturity and commercial potential, rather than precise identification." (2019, P2 Q3)
- On curveball wines: "No marks were given for attempts on identification." (2019, P1 Q3)

### Mark allocation signals required depth
- "Students need to be very mindful of the number of marks allocated to each element of the question." (2017, 2018)
- High mark values signal the need for detailed treatment; low mark values signal concise answers

### New question types appear regularly
- 2024: "Comment on the quality within the context of wine globally" — never asked before
- 2025: "Discuss the relative importance of human inputs vs nature" — never asked before
- Template answers will not suffice; the exam evolves

### Curveball wine marks are redistributed
- When a curveball is present, identification marks are downweighted
- Style, winemaking, quality, and commercial discussion absorb the marks
- This allows candidates to pass even with misidentification

### Wine selection balances classic and challenging
- "For every challenging question, there will be a correspondingly straightforward question." (2017)
- Paper 2 tends to have the most classic wines and the highest pass rate
- Paper 3 has the most stylistic diversity and tests breadth

## 7. Key Quotes for Mock Answer Writer Calibration

### On the purpose of the exam:
> "The ability to determine a wine's quality and value is arguably more important than being able to identify a wine blind." (2019)

### On what MWs must be:
> "Masters of Wine need to be generalists; it is not good enough to rely on expertise from a limited number of regions." (2018)

### On the exam's evolution:
> "As many wine styles around the world are more alike than different, the practical exam has evolved over the years and today it relies less on 'getting the wine right' and focuses more on theoretical aspects such as commercial appeal, winemaking, maturity and vintage." (2024)

### On the correct approach:
> "Taste like a detective; argue like a lawyer." (2019)

### On moderation criteria:
> "Reviewing all three papers allows one to clearly see strengths and weaknesses, whether the candidate shows convincing logic even if wrong, whether they recognize and clearly depict quality, style, and commercial aspects, and whether they can relate evidence from the glass to correctly distinguish winemaking techniques." (2023)
curveballAnalysis:
# Curveball Wine Analysis — MW Practical Exam 2011–2025

## Overview

Across 504 wines in 153 questions over 15 years:
- **31 wines (6.2%)** are high curveball
- **90 wines (17.9%)** are medium curveball
- **383 wines (75.9%)** are low curveball (standard/expected)

## Curveball Categories

### 1. Rare Variety (~35% of curveballs)
Uncommon grape varieties that most candidates cannot identify from sensory alone.

| Year | Paper | Wine | Variety | Context |
|------|-------|------|---------|---------|
| 2011 | P1 | Crios, Susana Balbo | Torrontes | 6-wine breadth flight |
| 2017 | P2 | Pietradolce Archineri | Nerello Mascalese | 4-wine same-country Italy |
| 2017 | P2 | Tiefenbrunner Castel Turmhof | Lagrein | 4-wine mixed varieties |
| 2018 | P1 | Pieropan La Rocca | Garganega | 6-wine mixed single varieties |
| 2019 | P2 | Bodega Garzon | Tannat | 4-wine Americas flight |
| 2021 | P3 | Contero | Brachetto | 5-wine different methods |
| 2024 | P1 | Gaia Wild Ferment | Moschofilero | 3-wine mixed aromatic varieties |
| 2025 | P2 | Alpha Estate Hedgehog | Xinomavro | 3-wine same-variety flight |

### 2. Rare Style (~30% of curveballs)
Unusual winemaking styles or production methods.

| Year | Paper | Wine | Style | Context |
|------|-------|------|-------|---------|
| 2014 | P3 | La Tour Vieille Banyuls Rimage | French VDN | 4-wine fortified flight |
| 2015 | P3 | Domaine de la Pinte Savagnin | Jura sous voile | 4-wine method comparison |
| 2015 | P3 | Kalleske JMK Shiraz VP | Australian fortified Shiraz | 4-wine fortified flight |
| 2016 | P1 | Basilio Izquierdo Acodo | Oxidative white Rioja | 2-wine same-country Spain |
| 2018 | P1 | Lopez de Heredia Gravonia | Oxidative white Rioja | 6-wine breadth |
| 2019 | P3 | Montbourgeau L'Etoile | Jura sous voile | 2-wine paired with Manzanilla |
| 2021 | P3 | Pouderoux Maury | Grenache-based VDN | 5-wine different methods |
| 2021 | P3 | Stanton & Killeen Rutherglen Muscat | Rutherglen fortified | 5-wine different methods |
| 2022 | P3 | Venturini Baldini Lambrusco Rosato | Lambrusco sparkling rosé | 4-wine sparkling rosé |
| 2024 | P1 | Domaine Castera Jurancon Sec | Obscure French AOC | 3-wine same-country France |
| 2024 | P3 | Domaine de Durban Muscat BdV | Muscat VDN | 5-wine RS flight |
| 2025 | P1 | Lopez de Haro Rioja Blanco GR | Oxidative aged white | 3-wine three countries |

### 3. Unexpected Origin (~20% of curveballs)
Well-known variety from an unusual country, or obscure sub-region.

| Year | Paper | Wine | Why Unexpected | Context |
|------|-------|------|----------------|---------|
| 2015 | P3 | Knightor Vineyard Rosé | English sparkling from Cornwall | 4-wine method comparison |
| 2018 | P3 | Birichino Bechthold Cinsault | Cinsault from Lodi, California | 3-wine Rhône varieties |
| 2021 | P1 | Botanica Mary Delany Chenin | Citrusdal Mountains, SA | 3-wine same-variety Chenin |
| 2025 | P2 | Tenuta di Arceno Aranum | Cab Franc as Super Tuscan IGT | 3-wine same-variety Cab Franc |

### 4. Hidden Identity (~15% of curveballs)
Wine where variety/origin is intentionally hard to identify because the wine is atypical of its type.

| Year | Paper | Wine | Why Hidden | Context |
|------|-------|------|------------|---------|
| 2012 | P2 | Dow Vale do Bomfim | Dry Douro red (not Port) | 4-wine mixed bag |
| 2018 | P1 | Mount Pleasant Lovedale Semillon | Aged Hunter Semillon (toasty, 10% ABV) | 6-wine breadth |
| 2021 | P3 | Henriques & Henriques Boal | Lesser-known Madeira grape | 4-wine fortified |
| 2022 | P1 | Tahbilk Museum Marsanne | Aged Australian Marsanne | 3-wine same-country Australia |
| 2025 | P1 | Gunther-Chereau Muscadet cru | Premium aged Muscadet (≠ cheap) | 3-wine same-country France |

## The Furmint/Tokaji Question (2023 P3 Q3)

The definitive curveball-as-pedagogical-tool example:

**Stem**: "Wines 8 and 9 are from the same region."
- a) Identify origin and grape varieties (14 marks)
- b) Compare production methods (16 marks — the main mark-driver)
- c) Comment on quality and maturity (2 × 10 marks)

**Wines**:
- Wine 8: **Furmint Tokaji, Dubogo, 2019** (13.5%) — DRY Furmint. The curveball.
- Wine 9: **Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos, Disznokó, 2013** (12%) — Sweet Aszú. The anchor.

**Why it works**: The dry Furmint is the curveball (most candidates associate Tokaj only with sweet wine). The Aszú is the anchor (recognizable benchmark sweet wine). The 16-mark production-method comparison is the heart — candidates must contrast reductive dry Furmint winemaking vs. botrytis/passerillage/oxidative Aszú aging. Even if a candidate can't name "Furmint," they can earn marks by describing the style contrast and inferring the region from Wine 9.

**Key pattern**: curveball is downweighted on identification marks and upweighted on style/method/quality discussion marks.

## Frequency Statistics

### Per paper average (high curveball only)
- Paper 1: **0.8** per paper
- Paper 2: **0.4** per paper
- Paper 3: **1.1** per paper

### Per question
- High curveball: 0.20 per question
- High + medium: 0.79 per question
- **18.3%** of questions contain at least one high curveball
- **44.4%** contain at least one high or medium curveball

### Ratio within questions
- 2-wine question: 1 curveball out of 2 (50%) — always paired with a clear anchor
- 3-wine question: 1 curveball out of 3 (33%)
- 4-wine question: 1 curveball out of 4 (25%) — **modal format**
- 5–6 wine question: 1–2 curveballs out of 5–6 (17–33%)

### By question type
- **Breadth/mixed-bag questions**: #1 host for curveballs
- **Same-country questions**: commonly include 1 curveball to test depth
- **Same-variety questions**: RARELY include curveballs (variety is stated)

### Price tier
- Commercial ($10–20): ~20% — Torrontes, Brachetto, Moschofilero, Lambrusco
- Specialist/premium ($20–50): ~65% — the bulk of curveballs
- Fine wine ($50+): ~15% — Franciacorta Bellavista, Colheita Madeira
- Almost no curveballs at luxury tier

## Examiner Deployment Patterns

1. **"1 in 4" rule**: In a multi-wine question, typically exactly 1 wine is significantly harder to identify. The others are anchors.

2. **Mark redistribution**: When a curveball is present, identification marks are often downweighted. The question upweights winemaking, quality, and commercial discussion — rewarding candidates who can describe what they taste even without naming the wine.

3. **Paper 3 concentration**: P3's mandate (sparkling, fortified, sweet, rosé, oxidative) inherently hosts more niche styles. Curveballs are most at home here.

4. **Never random**: Every curveball serves a pedagogical purpose within its question. The Tannat is in an Americas flight to test depth beyond Malbec/Carmenere. The dry Furmint is paired with Aszú to test understanding of Tokaj's modern evolution.

5. **Increasing frequency 2023–2025**: More deliberate curveball deployment (Moschofilero, Xinomavro, dry Furmint, Cab Franc IGT, Szamorodni) suggests the exam is expanding its varietal and stylistic scope.

6. **Curveball + anchor pairing**: In 2-wine curveball questions, the anchor wine (recognizable benchmark) provides the candidate a foothold. The curveball tests the edge of knowledge while the anchor tests the core. Example: Manzanilla (anchor) + Jura sous voile (curveball).
sourcingGuide:
# Mock Exam Sourcing Guide

*Generated from the historical corpus on 2026-05-26.*

## Why this exists

This guide makes the mock-exam writer more human and more exam-realistic. It should not blindly reuse the same producers or exact sourcing patterns from the past papers, but it should stay inside the historical sourcing envelope: benchmark regions, representative producers, realistic quality ladders, and plausible vintage freshness for a 2026 exam.

## Hard sourcing rules for 2026 mocks

- Use the historical corpus as a sourcing envelope, not a cloning list.
- Default to real, currently plausible market vintages for 2026.
- For young whites on Paper 1, prefer 2024-2025 vintages unless the question needs bottle development.
- For young reds on Paper 2, prefer 2023-2024 vintages unless the question needs development, oak integration, or mature tertiary structure.
- For Paper 3, let category drive vintage: NV for many sparkling/fortified wines, recent vintages for fresh aromatic or demi-sec wines, older vintages where oxidative or mature sweet styles are intrinsic.
- Within each question, deliberately vary quality tier, region, and commercial position when the marks ask for those distinctions.
- For quality-ranking questions, use the price-tier analysis as a guardrail: broad discrimination needs at least three price bands; all-premium flights need an explicit hierarchy hook.
- For Paper 1 production-method or winemaking questions, require a method-hook ledger before finalizing the flight: each wine needs a distinct discussable production hook, or at least one wine must be a clear method outlier. Do not ask production-method marks on three similar clean aromatic whites from the same region and tier.
- Use `data/mock_wine_bank.json` as the first external-candidate pool when the historical corpus is too narrow or recent mocks have overused a motif. Filter by `useful_families`, `style_category`, `price_band`, `quality_tier`, and cooldown motifs before naming wines.
- Default mix for new full mocks: at least 30% of named wines should come from the historical corpus, with the remaining selections drawn from `data/mock_wine_bank.json` or other validated external research. For a 36-wine exam, that means at least 11 historical wines and up to 25 bank-backed wines.
- Before finalizing any named mock wine, create a source-backed entry in `data/mock_wine_research/{mock_exam_id}.json`; do not let the answer writer invent vineyard, soil, producer, oak, price, or classification facts from memory.
- If the mock wine bank lacks a wine for the needed role, run wine research first and add a validated bank entry rather than letting the writer improvise.
- Run `python scripts/validate_mock_wine_facts.py --exam <mock.md> --answers <answers.md> --strict-research` before accepting a mock answer key.
- If you leave the corpus for a wine, research it first and mark it as external only if necessary.

## Quality / price-tier guardrail

Use `outputs/heuristics/quality_price_tier_analysis.md` when building quality-led questions.

- Historical price-band proxy distribution: premium=236, value=122, super_premium=85, luxury=36, mainstream=25
- Quality-led questions analysed: `134`
- Quality-led questions with three or more price bands: `23`
- Compressed all-high-priced quality questions: `60`
- Top-heavy quality questions: `35`

Exam-writer rule: if a question asks candidates to rank or distinguish quality, either build a visible ladder from value/mainstream to premium/fine wine, or make the stem explicitly about classification, same producer, same appellation, or another legally meaningful hierarchy.

## Quality / winemaking-diversity guardrail

Use `outputs/heuristics/winemaking_diversity_quality_questions.md` when a quality question also cites winemaking, method, production, oak, maturation, or human factors.

- Historical quality + winemaking/method questions analysed: `91`
- High diversity: `75`
- Moderate or paired/limited diversity: `8`
- Homogeneous winemaking signatures: `8`
- Same-variety homogeneous rate: `2` / `20`
- Compare/rank homogeneous rate: `1` / `18`

Exam-writer rule: if the stem asks for quality with reference to winemaking, a 3-wine flight needs at least two distinct winemaking signatures and a 4-wine flight needs at least three, unless the stem explicitly says classification, terroir, or same producer is the point. If the wines share the same production logic, ask quality with reference to origin/classification instead of winemaking.

## Full wine-selection scorecard guardrail

Use `outputs/heuristics/wine_selection_logic_by_question_type.md` and `scripts/build_mock_design_review.py` before finalizing a mock.

- Historical questions scored: `153`
- Historical fit statuses: strong=113, acceptable=23, fail=11, weak=6
- Family coverage: F1=33, F2=38, F3=7, F4=43, F5=16, F6=5, F7=11

Exam-writer rule: each question family has required active contrast axes. F1 needs expression spread; F2 needs internal-origin diversity; F3 needs blend-role differences; F4 needs independence; F5 needs method diversity; F6 needs structural mechanism spread; F7 needs a hierarchy or quality ladder.

## Cross-mock rotation guardrail

Use `outputs/mock_exams/mock_rotation_analysis.md` before selecting a new full mock blueprint.

- Full mocks analysed: `7`
- Historical Vouvray frequency: `7` / `504` wines
- Historical P3 Q1 sparkling frequency: `4` / `13` P3 Q1 questions
- Current repeated mock motifs: riesling=27, bordeaux=16, vouvray=13, huet=12, mosel=8, madeira=8

Exam-writer rule: never repeat the same paper/question family-subtype position in consecutive full mocks unless explicitly drilling. Rotate opening families, especially P3 Q1. Do not default P3 Q1 to three-country sparkling. Put lower-frequency motifs such as Vouvray, Huet/Foreau, Savennières, Tokaji, Rutherglen, Vin Santo, Etna, and Madeira on a two-mock cooldown after use.

## Validated mock wine bank

Use `data/mock_wine_bank.json` as the controlled candidate pool for new or underused wines, and check `outputs/mock_exams/mock_wine_bank_report.md` for current coverage.

- Validated entries: `94`
- Style coverage: still_dry=63, sparkling=9, fortified=8, still_sweet=6, oxidative=4, still_off_dry=2, orange=1, rose=1
- Price-band coverage: premium=43, super_premium=20, mainstream=18, luxury=10, value=3
- Quality-tier coverage: benchmark=45, iconic=28, regional=11, commercial=6, premium=4
- Family coverage: F1=62, F2=46, F3=12, F4=32, F5=42, F6=37, F7=91
- Top country coverage: France=27, Italy=12, Spain=9, Australia=9, Portugal=8, South Africa=6, Germany=5, Argentina=3, Chile=3, United States=3

Exam-writer rule: select from the bank by role, not by habit. Each chosen wine must match the question family, add a live contrast axis, avoid current cooldown motifs, and have source-backed facts copied into the mock-specific research file before the answer key is written.

## Paper-specific vintage logic

- Paper 1: freshness matters, so youthful examples should usually be 2024-2025; top-tier Chardonnay/Riesling/Chenin can be older when ageability or maturity is part of the question.
- Paper 2: youthful commercial reds should usually be 2023-2024; serious Nebbiolo, Rioja, Bordeaux blends, and structured Syrah/Sangiovese can be older where development is educationally useful.
- Paper 3: NV is normal for many sparkling and fortified wines; sweet/oxidative wines can be much older if that is part of category identity.

## Paper 1 - Whites

- Historical wines in corpus: `168`

### Top countries

- `France`: 63
- `Australia`: 21
- `New Zealand`: 13
- `USA`: 12
- `Spain`: 12
- `Italy`: 12
- `South Africa`: 10
- `Austria`: 9

### Top regions

- `Burgundy`: 17
- `Alsace`: 15
- `Loire`: 10
- `California`: 8
- `Marlborough`: 8
- `Loire Valley`: 6
- `Wachau`: 5
- `Rioja`: 5

### Top style categories

- `still_dry`: 156
- `still_sweet`: 6
- `oxidative`: 4
- `sparkling`: 1
- `orange`: 1

### Quality-tier proxy buckets

- `unspecified`: 88
- `mid_tier`: 51
- `upper_tier`: 18
- `benchmark_premium`: 9
- `commercial`: 2

### Age / vintage buckets

- `mature_7y_plus`: 134
- `developing_3_6y`: 33
- `nv`: 1

### Sourcing guidance

- Build questions around benchmark white families first: Chardonnay, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Chenin Blanc, Semillon, Pinot Gris, white blends.
- Make at least one question distinguish hierarchy or winemaking, not just raw grape ID.
- If a question asks for method of production or winemaking, give candidates real production hooks: oak/MLF/lees handling, sur lie, skin contact, oxidative handling, sweetness mechanism, wild ferment, age-release strategy, or a commercial-vs-premium cellar regime. A same-region aromatic trio such as Alsace Pinot Gris, Gewurztraminer, and Muscat is historically better as an ID/style/maturity question unless one wine has a clearly different method.
- Include a spread of tiers: at least one commercial or mid-tier foil, at least one benchmark premium wine, and at least one wine with genuine ageing or complexity value.
- Representative producers should feel exam-plausible: serious regional benchmarks, widely known estates, or credible premium commercial labels.

## Paper 2 - Reds

- Historical wines in corpus: `168`

### Top countries

- `France`: 50
- `Italy`: 27
- `USA`: 16
- `Spain`: 12
- `Australia`: 11
- `Chile`: 9
- `New Zealand`: 9
- `Argentina`: 8

### Top regions

- `Bordeaux`: 16
- `California`: 12
- `Tuscany`: 10
- `Burgundy`: 9
- `Rioja`: 8
- `Beaujolais`: 7
- `Piedmont`: 7
- `Rhône Valley`: 7

### Top style categories

- `still_dry`: 167
- `fortified`: 1

### Quality-tier proxy buckets

- `unspecified`: 94
- `mid_tier`: 57
- `upper_tier`: 10
- `benchmark_premium`: 7

### Age / vintage buckets

- `mature_9y_plus`: 126
- `developing_4_8y`: 40
- `young_0_3y`: 2

### Sourcing guidance

- Build around structurally distinct red families: Pinot Noir, Nebbiolo, Sangiovese, Tempranillo, Syrah/Shiraz, Bordeaux varieties and blends.
- Make the paper test tannin, oak, climate, and maturity, not just fruit profile.
- Include tier spread deliberately: one or two benchmark ageworthy wines, one value or entry-level contrast, and one mid-premium regional classic.
- Historical sourcing strongly favors classic red regions over novelty for novelty's sake.

## Paper 3 - Special

- Historical wines in corpus: `168`

### Top countries

- `France`: 55
- `Portugal`: 22
- `Italy`: 19
- `Spain`: 18
- `Germany`: 11
- `New Zealand`: 10
- `USA`: 10
- `Australia`: 9

### Top regions

- `Champagne`: 14
- `Bordeaux`: 12
- `Madeira`: 9
- `Mosel`: 9
- `Veneto`: 9
- `Douro`: 8
- `California`: 8
- `Burgundy`: 6

### Top style categories

- `fortified`: 41
- `sparkling`: 40
- `still_sweet`: 40
- `still_dry`: 31
- `rose`: 9
- `oxidative`: 4
- `orange`: 2
- `rosé`: 1

### Quality-tier proxy buckets

- `unspecified`: 107
- `mid_tier`: 39
- `upper_tier`: 14
- `benchmark_premium`: 8

### Age / vintage buckets

- `mature_9y_plus`: 85
- `nv`: 45
- `developing_4_8y`: 27
- `unknown`: 7
- `young_0_3y`: 4

### Sourcing guidance

- Category logic is primary: sparkling method, oxidative vs topped-up, sweetness mechanism, fortification route.
- Include at least one trap where sugar is present but not at dessert-wine scale, or where oxidation is category-defining rather than faulty.
- Make sure the wine mix spans commercial reality: benchmark category leaders plus at least one niche or sommelier-facing curveball.
- Be cautious with blend-family predictions here; category cues matter more than exact grape in many question structures.

## How the mock-exam writer should use this

- Start from the question structure you want to test.
- Choose the educational contrast needed: quality ladder, region contrast, winemaking contrast, maturity contrast, commercial contrast, or sweetness/fortification mechanism.
- Then choose wines that fit that contrast while staying inside the historical sourcing envelope.
- For full mocks, keep the selection mix roughly 30% historical corpus and 70% validated bank/external candidates unless a paper-specific pattern calls for a stronger historical tilt.
- Do not back-solve by copying historic producer choices. Instead, pick representative wines that a human exam setter could plausibly source in 2026.
- Use `data/wine_research/` first for historical wines and `data/mock_wine_bank.json` for validated external candidates. If a category gap appears, invoke wine research and add the result to the bank before finalizing the mock.
- Do not reuse Vouvray, Huet, Foreau, generic sparkling-openers, Madeira, or other cooldown motifs just because they are familiar; the bank exists to give the writer better options.
wineCompositionAnalysis:
# Question Wine Composition Analysis

How the IMW selects and balances wines within each question type. Derived from the complete 13-year corpus (2011-2025, excluding 2020).

---

## Part 1: Sweet Wine Flight Composition — Historical Deep Dive

### All P3 questions involving sweet, residual sugar, or sweetness-mechanism wines

Below is every P3 question that contains sweet wines as a central theme (not just a stray sweet wine appearing in a mixed-bag question). For each, I list the wines, their sweetness mechanism, variety, country, and approximate price tier.

---

#### 2012 P3 Q3: "Wines 7 and 8 are from different countries. Each is made from the same, single grape variety."
**Focus**: balance between acidity and sweetness (2 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W7 | Zeltinger Himmelreich Riesling Eiswein, Selbach Oster 2009 | **Ice wine (cryoextraction)** | Riesling | Germany | Premium |
| W8 | Kayena Botrytis Riesling, Tamar Ridge 2009 | **Botrytis** | Riesling | Australia | Mainstream |

- Distinct mechanisms: 2 (ice, botrytis)
- Variety repetition: YES — both Riesling (intentional: same-variety question)
- Price spread: moderate (~$40-80 range for both)
- Countries: 2

#### 2012 P3 Q4: "Wines 9-12 are all made from the same, single grape variety" (Muscat flight)
**Focus**: production techniques, alcohol level, quality and style (4 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W9 | Asti, Martini NV | **Stopped fermentation (Charmat/Asti method)** | Muscat | Italy | Value |
| W10 | Muscat VDN, Cuvée Réserve NV Rivesaltes | **Fortification (mutage)** | Muscat | France | Mainstream |
| W11 | Barefoot Moscato NV | **Stopped fermentation / low alcohol** | Muscat | USA | Value |
| W12 | Moscato Passito di Pantelleria Ben Rye 2009 | **Passerillage (dried grape)** | Muscat | Italy | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 3 (stopped fermentation x2, fortification, passerillage)
- Variety repetition: YES — all Muscat (intentional: same-variety question)
- NOTE: This is a same-variety question (F1d) — the repetition of Muscat IS the point. The IMW is testing knowledge of Muscat in different guises.
- Countries: 3

#### 2013 P3 Q3: "Wines 7-12 are from six different countries; none are fortified."
**Focus**: origin, method of production, residual sugar level, alcohol level (6 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W7 | Ürziger Würzgarten Riesling Kabinett, Dr Loosen 2009 | **Natural RS (arrested/cool ferment)** | Riesling | Germany | Mainstream |
| W8 | Jurancon, Château Jolys 2010 | **Late harvest / passerillage (Petit Manseng)** | Petit Manseng | France | Mainstream |
| W9 | Tokaji 5 Puttonyos Aszu, Royal Tokaji 2008 | **Botrytis (aszú)** | Furmint | Hungary | Premium |
| W10 | Cuvée Beerenauslese, Alois Kracher 2010 | **Botrytis (BA)** | Blend | Austria | Premium/Super-premium |
| W11 | Vidal Ice Wine, Peller Estate 2010 | **Ice wine (cryoextraction)** | Vidal | Canada | Premium |
| W12 | Vin Santo, Capezzana 2006 | **Passerillage (dried grape)** | Trebbiano/Malvasia | Italy | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 5 (natural RS, late harvest/passerillage, botrytis x2, ice wine, dried grape/Vin Santo)
- Variety repetition: NONE — 6 different varieties/blends
- Botrytis appears twice: Tokaji Aszú and Kracher BA — BUT these are very different expressions (Hungarian Furmint aszú vs Austrian Burgenland cuvée BA), different countries, different price points, different styles.
- Countries: 6 (all different)
- Price spread: well-distributed from ~$15-20 Kabinett to ~$40-80 range

**KEY INSIGHT**: When the IMW does repeat a mechanism (botrytis x2 here), they ensure maximum variety in every other dimension — different country, different grape, different style, different price level.

#### 2015 P3 Q3: "Wines 9-12 are from four different countries."
**Focus**: alcohol level, residual sugar, origin, method of production, quality (4 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W9 | Brauenberger Juffer-Sonnenuhr Riesling Spätlese, Richter 2008 | **Natural RS (Spätlese)** | Riesling | Germany | Premium |
| W10 | Riesling Icewine, Inniskillin 2012 | **Ice wine (cryoextraction)** | Riesling | Canada | Super-premium |
| W11 | Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos, Royal Tokaji 2008 | **Botrytis (aszú)** | Furmint | Hungary | Premium |
| W12 | Vin Santo, Isole e Olena 2005 | **Passerillage (dried grape)** | Trebbiano/Malvasia | Italy | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 4 (natural RS, ice, botrytis, passerillage) — ALL DIFFERENT
- Variety repetition: Riesling x2 (Germany Spätlese + Canada Icewine) — but used to illustrate radically different mechanisms on the same grape
- Countries: 4 (all different)
- Price spread: ~$25 Spätlese to ~$60-80 Icewine/Tokaji to ~$40 Vin Santo — reasonable spread

#### 2016 P3 Q3: "Wines 9 and 10 are from different countries."
**Focus**: method of production, quality (2 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W9 | Tokaji Aszú 6 Puttonyos, Royal Tokay 2011 | **Botrytis (aszú)** | Furmint | Hungary | Premium |
| W10 | Château Guiraud 1er Cru Classé 2001 | **Botrytis (noble rot)** | Sémillon/Sauvignon | France | Super-premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 1 (botrytis both) — BUT this is a 2-wine flight specifically designed to compare botrytis in two different traditions
- This is a compare-and-contrast question, not a breadth-of-mechanism question
- Countries: 2
- The IMW deliberately pairs same-mechanism wines ONLY when the question explicitly asks for comparison of that mechanism

#### 2019 P3 Q5: "Wines 11-12 come from two different countries."
**Focus**: compare/contrast method, residual sugar level, alcohol, quality (2 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W11 | Ben Ryé Passito de Pantelleria, Donnafugata 2016 | **Passerillage (dried grape)** | Muscat/Zibibbo | Italy | Premium |
| W12 | Muscat, Campbells NV Rutherglen | **Fortification (liqueur Muscat)** | Muscat | Australia | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 2 (passerillage, fortification)
- Variety: Both Muscat — but different mechanisms are the point
- Countries: 2
- This is a compare-and-contrast pair, and although both are Muscat, the mechanisms are completely different

#### 2021 P3 Q2: "Wines 4-8 are all made using different methods of production."
**Focus**: method of production with reference to RS and alcohol levels (5 wines, mixed sweet/fortified/dried)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W4 | Amarone della Valpolicella, Torre del Falasco 2015 | **Passerillage/appassimento (dried grape, dry wine)** | Corvina blend | Italy | Premium |
| W5 | Brachetto d'Aqui, Contero 2020 | **Stopped fermentation (low alc sweet)** | Brachetto | Italy | Value/Mainstream |
| W6 | Vintage Port, Graham's 2000 | **Fortification (mutage)** | Touriga blend | Portugal | Super-premium |
| W7 | Maury, Domaine Pouderoux NV | **Fortification (VDN)** | Grenache | France | Mainstream |
| W8 | Rutherglen Muscat Classic, Stanton & Killeen NV | **Fortification (liqueur Muscat)** | Muscat | Australia | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 4 (passerillage, stopped fermentation, fortification x3 — but Port/Maury/Rutherglen are very different fortification traditions)
- The stem EXPLICITLY says "different methods of production" — so the IMW sometimes groups wines that share a broad mechanism category (fortification) but considers Port, VDN, and Rutherglen Muscat as "different methods"
- Variety repetition: all different
- Countries: 4

**KEY INSIGHT**: The IMW considers Port fortification, Grenache-based VDN, and Rutherglen Muscat as distinct production methods even though all involve spirit addition. The level of granularity is at the production-tradition level, not just "fortified vs not."

#### 2022 P3 Q2: "Wines 5-9 are all from different countries... with specific reference to the level of residual sugar."
**Focus**: variety, origin, winemaking, RS, quality, maturity (5 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W5 | Gewürztraminer, Te Whare Ra 2020 | **Natural RS / off-dry** | Gewürztraminer | New Zealand | Premium |
| W6 | Riesling Spätlese, Schloss Reinhartshausen 2015 | **Natural RS (Spätlese)** | Riesling | Germany | Premium |
| W7 | T Noble Late Harvest Chenin Blanc, Ken Forrester 2019 | **Late harvest / possible botrytis** | Chenin Blanc | South Africa | Premium |
| W8 | Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos, Sauska 2017 | **Botrytis (aszú)** | Furmint | Hungary | Premium |
| W9 | Château Coutet 2016, Sauternes-Barsac | **Botrytis (noble rot)** | Sémillon | France | Super-premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 3 (natural RS off-dry, natural RS Spätlese, late harvest, botrytis x2)
- Botrytis repeated: Tokaji + Sauternes — different countries, different grapes, different traditions
- The flight includes a SPECTRUM from off-dry (Gewürz) through medium-sweet (Spätlese) to fully sweet (Tokaji, Sauternes)
- Variety repetition: NONE — all different
- Countries: 5 (all different)

#### 2024 P3 Q4: "Wines 8-12 all have residual sugar."
**Focus**: RS level, alcohol, origin, variety, winemaking (5 wines)

| Wine | Full ID | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Tier |
|------|---------|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| W8 | Vidal Icewine, Inniskillin 2021 | **Ice wine (cryoextraction)** | Vidal | Canada | Super-premium |
| W9 | Beerenauslese Cuvée, Kracher 2019 | **Botrytis (BA)** | Blend | Austria | Super-premium |
| W10 | Vin Santo del Chianti Classico, Badia a Coltibuono 2014 | **Passerillage (dried grape)** | Trebbiano/Malvasia | Italy | Premium |
| W11 | Muscat de Beaumes de Venise, Domaine de Durban 2020 | **Fortification (VDN mutage)** | Muscat | France | Mainstream |
| W12 | Clos du Bourg Demi-Sec, Domaine Huet 2020 | **Natural RS / late harvest (Chenin)** | Chenin Blanc | France | Premium |

- Distinct mechanisms: 5 (ice, botrytis, passerillage, fortification, natural RS) — ALL DIFFERENT
- Variety repetition: NONE — all different
- Countries: 4 (Canada, Austria, Italy, France x2 — two French wines but from completely different traditions: VDN vs Loire Chenin)
- Price spread: well-distributed (~$15-25 Beaumes de Venise to ~$30-50 Vin Santo/Huet to ~$100+ Kracher/Inniskillin)

**This is the gold standard for a sweet wine mechanism question.**

---

### Summary Statistics: Sweet Wine Flights in Historical P3

| Metric | Range | Average | Notes |
|--------|-------|---------|-------|
| Flight size | 2-6 wines | 3.8 | Smaller flights (2) test compare/contrast; larger (4-6) test breadth |
| Distinct mechanisms per flight | 1-5 | 3.3 | Only the 2016 botrytis compare-and-contrast pair has 1 mechanism |
| Mechanism repetition allowed? | Rarely | — | Only when the question explicitly tests comparison within one mechanism (2016), or the repeated mechanism has maximum other-dimension diversity (2013) |
| Distinct varieties per flight | 1-6 | 3.6 | Only repeated when same-variety is the explicit question structure (2012 Q3/Q4) |
| Countries per flight | 2-6 | 3.7 | The IMW strongly favors geographic spread |
| Price tier range | Moderate-wide | — | Never pairs extreme luxury with extreme value without 2+ wines in between |

### Rules Derived: Sweet Wine Flights

**RULE S1: Mechanism diversity is paramount.**
In any flight of 4+ sweet wines where mechanism is a question focus, the IMW uses at least 3 distinct mechanisms. In 5-wine flights, they aim for 4-5 distinct mechanisms. The 2024 flight achieves 5 for 5.

**RULE S2: If a mechanism repeats, every other dimension must differ.**
When botrytis appears twice (2013, 2022), the two botrytis wines are from different countries, different varieties, and different stylistic traditions. The IMW never places two wines with the same mechanism + same variety + similar price in the same mechanism-testing flight.

**RULE S3: Same-variety repetition is permitted only in explicitly same-variety questions.**
The 2012 Muscat flight (Q4) and 2012 Riesling pair (Q3) both repeat varieties because the question stem says "same grape variety." In mechanism-breadth questions, all varieties should differ.

**RULE S4: No extreme price tier gaps without bridging.**
The IMW does not pair a ~$2000 TBA with a ~$60 wine as the only two botrytis examples. When luxury wines appear, the flight includes wines across multiple price tiers to create a continuum.

**RULE S5: The sweetness spectrum should be represented.**
The best flights (2013, 2022, 2024) include wines from off-dry through medium-sweet to fully sweet to lusciously sweet. This tests the candidate's ability to calibrate RS levels.

**RULE S6: Fortification sub-traditions count as distinct methods.**
Port, Sherry, VDN (Muscat or Grenache), Madeira, and Rutherglen Muscat are treated as different production methods even though all involve spirit addition.

---

## Part 2: All Question Types — General Cross-Tabulation

### Flight size by paper and question family

| Paper | Typical flight size | Range | Notes |
|-------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| P1 | 2-6 wines | 2-6 | Most common: 2-4 |
| P2 | 2-6 wines | 2-6 | Most common: 3-4 |
| P3 | 2-6 wines | 1-6 | Most common: 2-4; single wine only in 2017 Q2 (orange wine) |

### Variety repetition by question type

**Same-variety questions (F1)**: Variety repetition is the entire point. All wines share one variety. This accounts for ~35% of all questions.

**Same-origin questions (F2)**: Variety repetition is rare but not prohibited. When the origin constraint is tight (same region), different varieties are expected. Approximately 5% of F2 questions repeat a variety.

**Mixed-identification questions (F4)**: Variety repetition is almost never seen. The stem typically says "different grape varieties." Less than 3% of F4 questions repeat a variety.

**Method/production questions (F5/F6)**: Variety repetition is acceptable when the production method, not the variety, is the testing point. ~15% of method questions use the same variety twice but in different production styles.

### Country distribution patterns

**Papers 1 and 2**: The IMW routinely concentrates wines in 1-2 countries for same-origin questions. For cross-origin questions, 3-6 countries is standard.

**Paper 3**: Geographic diversity is a hallmark. Even when wines share a style (all sparkling, all fortified), the IMW typically draws from 3+ countries. The only exception is same-producer flights (e.g., Dom Pérignon 1999/2009 in 2018; Madeira flights).

### Price tier distribution by question type

| Question Type | Value % | Mainstream % | Premium % | Super-premium % | Luxury % |
|--------------|---------|-------------|-----------|----------------|----------|
| F1 (same variety) | 10-15% | 25-30% | 35-40% | 15-20% | 0-5% |
| F2 (same origin) | 5-10% | 20-30% | 40-50% | 15-20% | 0-5% |
| F4 (mixed ID) | 10-20% | 30-40% | 30-40% | 5-15% | 0-2% |
| F5 (method) | 10-15% | 25-35% | 30-40% | 15-20% | 0-5% |
| F7 (hierarchy) | 5-10% | 15-25% | 30-40% | 20-30% | 5-10% |
| Sweet flights | 0-5% | 15-25% | 35-45% | 20-30% | 0-5% |

**Key finding**: The IMW rarely includes more than one luxury-tier wine per question. Hierarchy questions (F7) are the most likely to include a luxury wine, and even then it typically appears alongside a value or mainstream wine for contrast.

### Benchmark vs non-benchmark distribution

Across all papers:
- ~55-65% of wines are from benchmark regions/varieties (the top 20 that appear most frequently in the corpus)
- ~25-35% are from established but less common origins
- ~5-15% are genuine curveballs (unusual variety, unusual origin, or unusual style for the paper)

**Per question**: The IMW typically includes 0-1 curveball wines per question. A question with 2+ genuine curveballs is extremely rare (occurs ~5% of the time, almost exclusively in mixed-bag Q5-style questions).

---

## Part 3: Guardrails for Mock Exam Generation

These rules should be enforced by the mock-exam-writer agent when constructing any question.

### General rules (all papers, all question types)

**G1: Variety repetition within a question must be intentional.**
- In same-variety questions (F1): repetition is mandatory (that's the question)
- In method/style questions (F5/F6): allow at most 1 repeated variety, and only if the two wines differ sharply in style or mechanism
- In all other questions: no variety repetition unless the stem explicitly permits it

**G2: Price tier balance.**
- No more than 2 wines from the same price tier in a 4+ wine flight, unless the question is specifically a hierarchy/quality question
- Do not pair a luxury wine (>$200) with only value wines (<$15) in the same flight. Include at least one bridging wine in between
- The maximum price ratio in a sweet wine flight should not exceed ~20:1 (e.g., a $500 TBA is fine alongside a $25 Spätlese, but a $3000 TBA alongside a $15 Moscato is not)

**G3: Country diversity targets.**
- 2-wine flights: 2 countries (unless same-origin question)
- 3-wine flights: 2-3 countries
- 4-wine flights: 3-4 countries (unless same-origin)
- 5-6 wine flights: 4-6 countries (unless same-origin)
- Exception: same-origin questions can have all wines from one country

**G4: Curveball budget.**
- Maximum 1 curveball wine per question
- Maximum 2 curveball wines per paper
- A curveball should not appear in a same-variety question (it breaks the ID logic)

### Sweet wine flight specific rules

**S1: Mechanism diversity requirement.**
For any sweet wine flight of 4+ wines where "method of production" or "mechanism of sweetness" appears in the question:
- 4-wine flight: minimum 3 distinct mechanisms
- 5-wine flight: minimum 4 distinct mechanisms (target 5)
- 6-wine flight: minimum 5 distinct mechanisms

**S2: No same-mechanism + same-variety pairing.**
Never place two wines in the same flight that share BOTH the same sweetness mechanism AND the same grape variety, unless the question explicitly tests comparison within one tradition.

**S3: Mechanism taxonomy for counting.**
Count these as distinct mechanisms:
1. **Ice wine / cryoextraction** (natural freeze or cryo)
2. **Botrytis / noble rot** (Sauternes, BA, TBA, Tokaji Aszú, Alsace SGN)
3. **Passerillage / dried grape** (Vin Santo, Passito, Recioto, Amarone-adjacent, Jurançon sec-appassimento)
4. **Fortification — Port tradition** (Vintage Port, Ruby, Tawny, LBV)
5. **Fortification — VDN/Muscat tradition** (Beaumes de Venise, Rivesaltes, Rutherglen Muscat, Banyuls)
6. **Fortification — Sherry tradition** (Oloroso dulce, Cream, PX)
7. **Fortification — Madeira tradition** (estufagem/canteiro)
8. **Natural RS / stopped fermentation** (German Prädikat, off-dry Riesling, Moscato d'Asti, demi-sec Vouvray, Brachetto)
9. **Late harvest** (Spätlese, Vendanges Tardives, Late Harvest without botrytis)
10. **Chaptalisation-adjacent** (theoretical — extremely rare in the corpus)

For the purpose of the "distinct mechanism" count:
- Items 4-7 are all "fortification" at the broadest level but the IMW treats them as distinct methods
- Items 2 and 9 both involve overripe grapes, but botrytis is a distinct fungal process vs simple late harvest
- Items 1 and 2 both concentrate sugars naturally but by radically different processes

**S4: Price tier spread in sweet flights.**
Sweet wine prices vary enormously, but the IMW has a hard practical ceiling. The exam needs ~25 bottles per wine; the most expensive wine ever poured was approximately $300-400/bottle (Batard-Montrachet Grand Cru, Dom Perignon 1999). The most expensive sweet wine ever used was Kracher BA at ~$80-120. No Egon Muller TBA ($2000+), no Yquem ($300+), no Donnhoff Oberhäuser Brücke ($200+).
- Hard cap: no wine above ~$400 retail in any mock exam
- For sweet flights specifically: max ~$120 per wine (historical sweet wine ceiling)
- The most expensive wine should not be more than 20x the price of the cheapest wine
- If including a super-premium sweet wine (~$80-120), include at least 2 wines in the $20-60 range
- The IMW typically uses mid-range premium sweet wines as the backbone ($30-80)

**S5: Sweetness level spectrum.**
A good sweet wine flight should span a range of residual sugar levels:
- Off-dry / light sweet: 10-30 g/l (e.g., Spätlese, off-dry Gewürz, demi-sec)
- Medium sweet: 30-80 g/l (e.g., Auslese, Late Harvest)
- Sweet: 80-150 g/l (e.g., Sauternes, Aszú 5 Putt)
- Lusciously sweet: 150+ g/l (e.g., TBA, Eiswein, Aszú 6 Putt)
- Fortified sweet: variable RS but with fortification context

Include at least 3 different sweetness levels in a 5-wine flight.

### Fortified wine flight specific rules

**F1: Fortified flights should span traditions.**
The IMW never puts 3 wines from the same fortified category in one flight unless the question is specifically about that category (e.g., 2014 P3 Q2 — four fortified wines from four different traditions: Sherry, Madeira, Port, Banyuls).

**F2: Within fortified flights, vary the style axis.**
If using multiple fortified wines, vary along: dry vs sweet, oxidative vs reductive, young vs aged, grape-driven vs process-driven.

### Sparkling wine flight specific rules

**SP1: Method diversity in sparkling flights.**
Non-Champagne sparkling questions should include at least 2 different sparkling methods (Traditional, Charmat, Transfer, Ancestral).

**SP2: Quality spread in sparkling flights.**
Include at least one value/commercial and one premium/artisan sparkling to test calibration.

---

## Part 4: V6 Mock Exam Evaluation — P3 Q3

### The v6 selection

The v6 mock P3 Q3 presents 5 wines "from five different countries... with the presence of residual sugar" and asks about "the mechanism by which sweetness has been achieved":

| Wine | Mechanism | Variety | Country | Approx Price |
|------|-----------|---------|---------|-------------|
| W8: Muscat de Beaumes-de-Venise, Durban 2023 | Fortification (VDN) | **Muscat** | France | ~$20-30 |
| W9: Muscat Liqueur, Chambers Rosewood NV Rutherglen | Fortification (Liqueur Muscat) | **Muscat** | Australia | ~$25-40 |
| W10: Riesling TBA, Egon Müller Scharzhof 2016 | Botrytis (TBA) | Riesling | Germany | **~$2,000-5,000** |
| W11: Tokaji Aszú 6 Puttonyos, Royal Tokaji 2017 | Botrytis (Aszú) | Furmint | Hungary | ~$50-80 |
| W12: Riesling Icewine, Inniskillin 2022 | Ice wine | Riesling | Canada | ~$50-80 |

### Violations identified

**Violation 1: Same variety repeated — Muscat x2 (SEVERE)**
W8 and W9 are both Muscat. In a 5-wine mechanism question, the IMW has NEVER repeated a variety in the historical corpus (2013, 2015, 2022, 2024 — all 4+ wine sweet flights have all-distinct varieties). Rule G1 violated.

**Violation 2: Same mechanism repeated — Fortification x2 with same grape (SEVERE)**
W8 (VDN Muscat) and W9 (Rutherglen Muscat) are both fortified Muscat wines. While the IMW does sometimes consider VDN and Rutherglen as "different methods" (2021 P3 Q2), that 2021 question explicitly said "different methods of production" and used different grapes for the two fortified-Muscat entries plus there were 3 other fortified wines alongside. Here, 2 of 5 wines share both mechanism (fortification) and variety (Muscat). No historical precedent for this in a mechanism-testing question.

**Violation 3: Same mechanism repeated — Botrytis x2 (MODERATE)**
W10 (TBA) and W11 (Tokaji Aszú) are both botrytis wines. This is not unprecedented — 2013 and 2022 both have botrytis appearing twice. However, in those cases, the flight had 5-6 wines with 4-5 total mechanisms. Here, with only 3 distinct mechanisms out of 5 wines (fortification, botrytis, ice), the mechanism diversity is below the historical floor.

**Violation 4: Only 3 distinct mechanisms in a 5-wine mechanism question (SEVERE)**
Mechanisms present: fortification (x2), botrytis (x2), ice wine (x1). That's 3 distinct mechanisms for 5 wines. Historical comparison:
- 2013 (6 wines): 5 mechanisms
- 2015 (4 wines): 4 mechanisms (all different)
- 2022 (5 wines): 3-4 mechanisms
- 2024 (5 wines): 5 mechanisms (all different)
The v6 mock is at the absolute floor and below the typical standard. Missing: passerillage/dried grape, natural RS/late harvest, stopped fermentation.

**Violation 5: Extreme price gap (SEVERE)**
Egon Müller TBA ($2,000-5,000) alongside wines in the $20-80 range. The price ratio is 50:1 to 250:1, far exceeding the historical maximum of ~20:1. The IMW has NEVER included a wine in the $2000+ range in a mechanism-testing flight. In the 2013 flight (arguably the closest to a luxury sweet wine with Kracher BA at ~$80-120), no wine exceeds ~$120.

**Violation 6: Missing key mechanisms (MODERATE)**
The flight has no representation of:
- Passerillage/dried grape (Vin Santo, Passito, Recioto) — appears in 4 of 5 historical sweet flights
- Natural RS / stopped fermentation (Spätlese, Moscato, demi-sec) — appears in 3 of 5 historical sweet flights
- Late harvest without botrytis — appears in 2 of 5 historical sweet flights

These are among the most commonly tested mechanisms.

### How the IMW would actually structure this question

Based on the historical patterns, a 5-wine sweet mechanism flight should look like:

**Example reconstruction (mechanism-diverse)**:
1. Riesling Spätlese, [German producer] — **Natural RS** (Germany, ~$20-30)
2. Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos, [Hungarian producer] — **Botrytis** (Hungary, ~$40-60)
3. Vidal Icewine, [Canadian producer] — **Ice wine** (Canada, ~$50-80)
4. Vin Santo del Chianti, [Italian producer] — **Passerillage** (Italy, ~$30-50)
5. Muscat de Beaumes de Venise, [French producer] — **Fortification/VDN** (France, ~$15-25)

Result: 5 mechanisms, 5 varieties, 5 countries, price range $15-80 (ratio ~5:1). This mirrors the 2024 actual exam flight almost exactly.

### Severity summary

| Violation | Severity | Historical precedent for violation |
|-----------|----------|------------------------------------|
| Muscat x2 (same variety) | HIGH | Never in mechanism questions |
| Fortification x2 + same grape | HIGH | Never |
| Botrytis x2 | MODERATE | Seen 2x but with compensating diversity |
| Only 3 mechanisms in 5 wines | HIGH | Below historical floor |
| $2000+ TBA in mechanism flight | HIGH | Never |
| Missing passerillage/natural RS | MODERATE | Present in 4/5 historical flights |

**Overall assessment**: The v6 P3 Q3 has 3 high-severity and 2 moderate-severity violations against historical patterns. It should be rebuilt from scratch using the guardrails above.
geographicVocabularyRules: The examiners use a strict abstract hierarchy: "country", "region", "sub-region", "area", "origin". They NEVER name specific geographic features, landforms, valleys, rivers, mountain ranges, or appellations in question stems. In 10 years of papers (2011-2025, 112 questions), the only geographic proper noun in a stem is "Rhône Valley" (2017 P2 Q4), used as a region name. NEVER use "broad valley", "river valley", "mountain range", "coastal region", "volcanic soils", "lake shore", or "appellation".

p1.text:
Wines 1 to 3 are from the same region of origin, but made from different single grape varieties.
For all three wines:
a) Identify the region of origin (15 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the grape variety and comment on the level of quality within the context of the region of origin (3 x 15 marks)
c) Comment on the age / vintage of each wine and its potential to develop and improve further (3 x 5 marks)

Wines 4 to 6 are made from the same grape variety.
For all three wines:
a) Identify the grape variety (15 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the origin as closely as possible and comment on the level of quality within the context of that origin (3 x 15 marks)
c) Discuss how the wine has been made, with specific reference to the use of oak and malolactic fermentation (3 x 5 marks)

Wines 7 to 12 are a mixed bag. Each comes from a different country and a different (predominant) grape variety.
For each wine:
a) Identify the predominant grape variety (6 x 7 marks)
b) Identify the country and region of origin as closely as possible (6 x 8 marks)
c) Comment on the level of quality (6 x 10 marks)

Wines 1-4 are all from the same region of origin.
For each wine:
a) Identify the specific origin as closely as possible (4 x 10\)
b) Identify the grape variety(ies) (4 x 6\)
c) Comment on the winemaking (4 x 3\)
d) Discuss the quality with reference to state of maturity (4 x 6\)

Wines 5-7 are all made from the same single grape variety and come from different countries.
With reference to all three wines:
a) Identify the grape variety (15)
Then for each wine:
b) Identify the origin as closely as possible (3 x 10\)
c) Discuss the quality with particular reference to winemaking techniques employed (3 x 10\)

Wines 8-10 are all from the same region and are from the same vintage.
For each wine:
a) Identify the specific origin as closely as possible (3 x 10\)
With reference to all three wines:
b) Compare the quality of the wines, within the context of the region of origin (30)
c) Identify the vintage (15)

p2.text:
Wines 1 to 3 are from the same country of origin.
For all three wines:
a) Identify the country of origin (15 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the grape variety/ies (3 x 10 marks)
c) Comment on the level of quality (3 x 10 marks)

Wines 4 and 5 are made from the same single grape variety and are from the same region of origin.
For both wines:
d) Identify the grape variety and region of origin as closely as possible (20 marks)
For each wine:
e) Comment on the method of production (2 x 5 marks)
f) Comment on quality and style, within the context of the region of origin (2 x 10 marks)

Wines 6 to 8 are made from different single grape varieties and are from the same region of origin.
For all three wines:
a) Identify the region of origin as closely as possible (24 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the grape variety (3 x 8 marks)
c) Comment on the method of production (3 x 4 marks)
d) Comment on quality, within the context of the region of origin (3 x 5 marks)

Wines 9 and 10 are from different countries.
For each wine:
a) Identify the grape variety/ies and region of origin as closely as possible (2 x 10 marks)
b) Comment on the winemaking, with particular reference to the use of oak (2 x 10 marks)
c) Identify the vintage and state of maturity (2 x 5 marks)

Wines 11 and 12 are both made from the same single grape variety, from different countries.
For both wines:
a) Identify the grape variety (20 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the origin as closely as possible (2 x 5 marks)
c) Comment on quality and style, with particular reference to commercial appeal (2 x 10 marks)

Wines 1-6 are from 6 different countries. All are made from one or more of the red varieties permitted in Bordeaux. The same variety may feature more than once.
For each wine:
a) Identify the region of origin and grape variety(ies) (6 x 12\)
b) Comment on the quality (within the context of the region of origin) and maturity (6 x 8\)
c) Comment on the winemaking (6 x 5\)

p3.text:
Wines 1 and 2 are from the same region and same producer.
With reference to both wines:
a) Identify the region of origin as closely as possible (10 marks)
b) Compare the most relevant winemaking techniques involved in the production of the wines (20 marks)
c) Compare maturity and quality, stating which is the higher quality wine (20 marks)

Wines 3-6 are each from a different country. Each is made predominantly from a different, single grape variety.
For each wine:
a) Identify the region of origin and the grape variety (4 x 15 marks)
b) To whom is this wine most likely to appeal, and why? (4 x 10 marks)

Wines 7 and 8 are from different countries. Each is made from the same, single grape variety.
With reference to both wines:
a) Identify the grape variety (10 marks)
b) Compare the quality and methods of production. Discuss the balance between acidity and sweetness in these wines (30 marks)
Then for each wine:
c) Identify the origin as closely as possible (2 x 5 marks)

Wines 9-12 are all made from the same, single grape variety.
For all four wines:
a) Identify the grape variety, with reference to each wine (12 marks)
For each wine:
b) Identify the most relevant production techniques evident in the wine (4 x 10 marks)
c) State the alcohol level (4 x 5 marks)
d) Comment on quality and style (4 x 7 marks)

Wines 1-3 are all from the same country.
For all three wines
a) Identify the country and regions of origin. (24 marks)
For each wine
c) Comment on the method of production. (7 marks)
d) Discuss the quality with particular reference to commercial potential. (10 marks)

Wines 4-6 are from two different countries. One grape variety is common to all three wines, but in varying proportions.
For each wine
a) Identify the region of origin and grape variety(ies). (10 marks)
b) Comment on the method of production. (7 marks)
c) Comment on quality and maturity. (8 marks)
